excerpt: To seek refuge through (bi-) the Prophet and the Imām is to place oneself under their spiritual protection and guidance, shielding oneself from false beliefs and corruption.
source_authority: Kirmani, chief Fatimid theorist of intellection
source_location_hint: Esoteric commentary on the stations of the Prophet and the Imāms

excerpt: Ziyārah (visiting) implies voluntary meeting and free choice, distinct from the forced carrying away of the dead to the grave.
source_authority: Chief jurist and esotericist of the Fatimid state
source_location_hint: On the meanings of death and the voluntary vs. compelled soul

excerpt: Man becomes immersed in greed and worldly desires, forgetting himself and neglecting all thought of the Hereafter and spiritual return.
source_location_hint: On the spiritual death and the stages of the soul's ascent

excerpt: With whomever a person aligns themselves in love and affection, with that one shall they be resurrected and gathered on the Day of Judgment.
source_authority: Sayings of Amīr al-Muʾminīn ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: On the principle of resurrection according to one's attachments

excerpt: Blessing (naʿīm) is explained as the cold water given to people on the Day when the heat is most intense, representing divine mercy and sustenance.
source_authority: Chief dāʿī of the Fatimid daʿwa
source_location_hint: 7th Session (Majlis): On the meaning of divine blessings and the questioning on the Day of Judgment

excerpt: Mount Sinai conceals a hidden secret—the reality of Jesus Christ and his role in extracting and manifesting the inner truths of the Torah.
source_authority: Chief dāʿī of the Fatimid daʿwa and esoteric teacher
source_location_hint: On the allegorical meanings of the oaths in Sūrat al-Tīn and the station of Jesus

excerpt: The Messenger of God sent forth Prophethood and Resurrection as twins, joined together without temporal separation.
source_authority: Chief dāʿī of the Fatimid state
source_location_hint: Poetic testimony to the simultaneity of the prophethood and the final hour

excerpt: The ranks within the daʿwa are organized as follows: those who extract knowledge (al-Nāziʿāt), those who bind by covenant (al-Nāshiṭāt), those immersed in knowledge (al-Sābihāt), those who advance others (al-Sābiqāt), and those who manage all affairs (al-Mudbbirāt), with the Imām as the Master of Command.
source_location_hint: On the hierarchical structure of the sacred summons and the stations of its officers

excerpt: Mawlānā Aḥmad ibn Ṭayyib, the twenty-third Imām, authored the Alif—a collection spanning a thousand years of divine teachings and verses (Surat).
source_location_hint: On the station of the Imāms and the transmission of divine knowledge through the generations

excerpt: The proof (ḥujja) of Mawlānā Muḥammad ibn Aḥmad is superior to a thousand other proofs, reflecting the unique station of this Imām in the hierarchy of divine guidance.
source_location_hint: On the ranks of the Imāms and the superiority of certain stations in the hierarchy

excerpt: The proclamations (surrat) continue throughout the night of concealment until the coming of the Dawn (al-Fajr), which is the manifestation of the Qāʾim, the Establisher of the Hour.
source_authority: Early Ismaili theologian and teacher of Qadi al-Nuʿman
source_location_hint: On the continuation of divine guidance through the ages and the awaited return
